statement.p: Think of a number. Add four to your number. Now double that. Then add six. Then halve it. Finally, subtract 7\text{.} What is the result? Do you always get the same result, regardless of what number you start with? How does this work? Explain using algebra.

solution.p: Lets start with the number x and follow the instructions: x
Add four to the number: x + 4
Thus, after following the instructions, you always end up with the original number.

webwork-reps.pg:
############################################# ### Generated from PreTeXt source ### ### https://pretextbook.org ### ############################################# ## DBsubject() ## DBchapter() ## DBsection() ## Level() ## KEYWORDS() ## TitleText1() ## EditionText1() ## AuthorText1() ## Section1(not reported) ## Problem1(A.4.6.63) ## Author() ## Institution() ## Language(en-US)
DOCUMENT();
############################################################ # Load Macros ############################################################ loadMacros( "PGstandard.pl", "PGML.pl", "AnswerFormatHelp.pl", "PGessaymacros.pl", "PGcourse.pl", ); COMMENT('Authored in PreTeXt');
############################################################ # Header ############################################################ TEXT(beginproblem());
############################################################ # PG Setup Code ############################################################ Context('Numeric');
############################################################ # Body ############################################################
BEGIN_PGML Think of a number. Add four to your number. Now double that. Then add six. Then halve it. Finally, subtract [`7\text{.}`] What is the result? Do you always get the same result, regardless of what number you start with? How does this work? Explain using algebra.
[@ANS(essay_cmp()); essay_box(6,76)@]* [@essay_help()@]*
END_PGML
############################################################ # Solution ############################################################
BEGIN_PGML_SOLUTION Let's start with the number [`x`] and follow the instructions: [`x`]
Add four to the number: [`x + 4`]
Now double that: [` 2(x+4)`]
Then add six: [` 2(x+4)+6`]
Then halve it: [` \frac{2(x + 4)+ 6}{2} `]
Finally, subtract 7: [` \frac{2(x + 4)+ 6}{2}-7 `]
This expression can be simplified:
[```\newcommand{\amp}{&} \begin{aligned} \frac{2(x + 4)+ 6}{2}-7 \amp = \frac{2x+8+6}{2} - 7\\ \amp = \frac{2x+14}{2}-7\\ \amp = x + 7 - 7\\ \amp = x \end{aligned} ```]
Thus, after following the instructions, you always end up with the original number.
END_PGML_SOLUTION
############################################################ # End Problem ############################################################
ENDDOCUMENT();
